moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Polish Opening, an unconventional first move that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside and prepares to fianchetto our light-squared bishop. While b4 doesn't control the center directly, it challenges Black's spatial advantage and sets up the plan of Bb2, where our bishop will eye the long diagonal toward g1-a7. This opening rewards understanding pawn structure and piece placement over forcing tactics, making it ideal for patient, positional players.

Black chooses a solid, flexible response that prepares d5 while supporting the center. The move e6 keeps our options open—Black can still play French-like or Indian-like structures depending on how we develop. By not committing fully to the center yet, Black maintains flexibility and avoids giving us clear tactical targets in the opening.

We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it exerts subtle pressure on e5 and d4. From b2, our bishop controls important central squares and will support our future kingside expansion. This move also connects our rooks and gives our position a solid, harmonious structure—a hallmark of the Polish Opening's patient, positional approach.

Black develops naturally, placing the knight on its ideal square while attacking our e4 square (even though we haven't played there yet) and keeping options open for both d5 and c6. The move prepares castling and prevents us from easily establishing control in the center. Black is keeping the position flexible and solid.

We aggressively advance on the queenside, fixing Black's structure and gaining space. By pushing b5, we're committing to our plan: create imbalance on the queenside where our bishop on b2 provides support, while we'll eventually develop our kingside pieces to control the center. This advance also prevents Black's typical b7-b6 setup, forcing them to react to our initiative.

Black stabilizes the queenside by supporting the a7-pawn and preventing our pawn from advancing further without cost. Playing b6 also prepares a bishop fianchetto on b7, mirroring our setup and establishing control of the long diagonal. Black is choosing solid, symmetrical development—maintaining the balance while preparing to challenge our bishop on b2 with their own.
